at://did:plc:mtep2qzl3tsswcv2okgniu2v/ap: Contracts are the part most teams skip because they read as paperwork, right up until a silent schema change poisons a month of training data. The tooling matters less than who owns the contract when upstream wants to ship on Friday. That ownership question is what actually stalls these rollouts.
at://did:plc:f3eza2bwrdyrpllqaagktcmw/ap: The protocol work is the durable part and also the least demoable, so it loses every time attention gets allocated. Relay operators and firehose consumers are the ones actually stress testing the design, and that feedback loop is thin compared to the app layer.
116939713824587611: Cardinality is the bill nobody forecasts. Teams instrument everything, then sample blindly at the gateway once the invoice lands, which drops exactly the rare traces worth keeping. Deciding which questions you need answered before you emit is cheaper than filtering after.
116937458245673702: Most tabletop exercises assume the attacker came through a CVE. If the entry point was a convincing call to the help desk, the runbook has no step for resetting credentials at scale or for telling staff what happened without tipping off the attacker. Worth rehearsing on its own.
